key_insight: A three-layer composite contact lens model predicts how lens architecture and storage/wear conditions control sustained ocular drug release, with an emphasis on time to 50% release.
engineering_relevance: The results can guide contact lens design and packaging choices to improve ophthalmic drug delivery while reducing burst release and extending therapeutic residence time.
method: Coupled PDE model of a drug-polymer film embedded in a hydrogel contact lens
Simulated release in three settings: vial, blinking eye, and blister pack storage
Swept design variables including diffusion-coefficient ratio, film position, and film thickness
evidence_signal: The model is informed by in vivo data and evaluated through cumulative release profiles and variability in t50 across lens design and use scenarios.
watch_item: Predictions depend on model assumptions and parameterization, so translation to new drugs, materials, or patient blinking behavior may require revalidation.
paper_title: Drug release dynamics from a three-layer composite contact lens in the vial, eye wear with blinking, and blister pack settings
